title: Emergency Guide: Cat Dragging Hind Legs and Unable to Stand
meta_desc: If your cat is dragging its back legs, it is a critical medical emergency. Learn about potential causes like saddle thrombus and next steps.
content_html:
What is happening to your cat?

Observing your cat suddenly losing the use of their hind legs is a distressing experience for any pet owner. This condition is most frequently associated with a Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), commonly referred to as a saddle thrombus. This occurs when a blood clot lodges at the base of the aorta, cutting off blood flow to the back legs. Other potential causes include severe spinal injury, slipped discs, or sudden neurological collapse.

Is this a medical emergency?

Yes. This is a high-priority, life-threatening emergency. If your cat is unable to stand, dragging their legs, or if their back paws feel cold to the touch, you must transport them to an emergency veterinary hospital immediately. This condition is extremely painful and requires professional intervention to manage pain and attempt to restore circulation.

Why a photo or video helps triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your departure, taking a brief video of your cat's attempts to move or a clear photo of their paw pads can be incredibly helpful. This visual evidence allows the veterinary triage team to quickly assess the nature of the paralysis or weakness, helping them prepare the necessary equipment and medication before you even walk through the door.

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if they aren't crying?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats are experts at masking pain, but hind-limb paralysis—especially from a blood clot—is considered one of the most painful conditions a cat can experience.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relief?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications as they are often toxic to felines. Immediate professional veterinary care is the only safe way to manage their pain.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a saddle thrombus?
faqs.answer: Common signs include sudden paralysis of the back legs, vocalizing in distress, cold back paws, and bluish or pale nail beds or paw pads due to lack of blood flow.
